metadata.title: Benefit-Risk Assessment for New Drug and Biological Products: Guidance for Industry

rules.rule_text: For a new drug to be approved for marketing in the United States, FDA must determine that the drug is safe and effective for use under the conditions prescribed, recommended, or suggested in the product's labeling. The demonstration of safety requires a showing that the benefits of the drug outweigh its risks.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — Regulatory Background
rules.verbatim_quote: Under the FD&C Act, for a new drug to be approved for marketing in the United States, FDA must determine that the drug is safe and effective for use under the conditions prescribed, recommended, or suggested in the product's labeling. Because all drugs can have adverse effects, the demonstration of safety requires a showing that the benefits of the drug outweigh its risks.
rules.exceptions: Biological products under PHS Act must demonstrate safety, purity, and potency (21 CFR 600.3(s))

rules.rule_text: Benefit-risk assessment is integrated into FDA's regulatory review of marketing applications and constitutes an informed judgment as to whether the benefits (with their uncertainties) of the drug outweigh the risks (with their uncertainties and approaches to managing risks) under the conditions of use described in the approved product labeling.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — Regulatory Background
rules.verbatim_quote: Broadly speaking, benefit-risk assessment in FDA's drug regulatory context is making an informed judgment as to whether the benefits (with their uncertainties) of the drug outweigh the risks (with their uncertainties and approaches to managing risks) under the conditions of use described in the approved product labeling.

rules.rule_text: A favorable benefit-risk assessment requires determining that the drug's benefits and risks are sufficiently characterized and that benefits to the intended population will outweigh the risks if the product is approved. This requires a thorough assessment of the extensive evidence of safety and effectiveness submitted by a sponsor in an NDA or BLA, as well as a thorough understanding of the data gaps.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — Regulatory Background
rules.verbatim_quote: Making an informed judgment that a drug has a favorable benefit-risk assessment requires determining that the drug's benefits and risks are sufficiently characterized and that the benefits to the intended population will outweigh the risks if the product is approved. FDA's benefit-risk assessment of a product for its proposed indication is a case-specific determination that requires a thorough assessment of the extensive evidence of safety and effectiveness submitted by a sponsor in an NDA or BLA, as well as a thorough understanding of the data gaps.

rules.rule_text: FDA's benefit-risk assessment comprises a case-specific, multi-disciplinary assessment that considers: (1) the therapeutic context including the nature and severity of the disease; (2) the evidence from clinical, non-clinical, patient experience, and epidemiologic data; (3) the uncertainties about the drug's benefits and risks; and (4) FDA's regulatory options to manage risks and reduce uncertainties.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — Regulatory Background
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA's benefit-risk assessment comprises a case-specific, multi-disciplinary assessment of science and medicine, which considers: The therapeutic context in which the drug will be used... The evidence submitted in the premarket application and/or generated in the postmarket setting... The uncertainties about the drug's benefits and risks... FDA's regulatory options to manage risks and to further reduce uncertainties.

rules.rule_text: FDA uses the Benefit-Risk Framework as its vehicle for conducting and communicating benefit-risk assessments. The Framework provides a structured, qualitative approach for identifying, assessing, and communicating the important considerations that factor into the benefit-risk assessment, including Analysis of Condition, Current Treatment Options, Benefit, Risk and Risk Management, and Conclusions Regarding Benefit-Risk.
rules.source_section: Section II.B — FDA's Benefit-Risk Framework
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA's vehicle for conducting and communicating its benefit-risk assessments is the Benefit-Risk Framework for new drug review. The Benefit-Risk Framework (Figure 1) provides a structured, qualitative approach for identifying, assessing, and communicating the important considerations that factor into the benefit-risk assessment.

rules.rule_text: The Benefit-Risk Framework includes two dimensions concerning the therapeutic context: Analysis of Condition and Current Treatment Options. It includes product-specific dimensions for Benefit and Risk and Risk Management. The framework integrates these in Conclusions Regarding Benefit-Risk. Each dimension requires both Evidence and Uncertainties and Conclusions and Reasons.
rules.source_section: Section II.B — FDA's Benefit-Risk Framework
rules.verbatim_quote: The first two rows in Figure 1 outline the important dimensions of the assessment concerning the therapeutic context, including Analysis of Condition and Current Treatment Options, followed by the product-specific rows for the assessment of Benefit and Risk and Risk Management. The columns distinguish two important inputs to each dimension: The Evidence and Uncertainties that are most pertinent to the benefit-risk assessment and the Conclusions and Reasons based on the evidence and its strength.

rules.rule_text: CDER has integrated the Benefit-Risk Framework into its clinical review and decisional memo templates since 2015. For marketing applications (NDA and BLA) assessments, the Benefit-Risk Framework appears as a component of section 1, Executive Summary. CBER integrates benefit-risk assessment through interdisciplinary review and incorporates the Framework into new BLA and supplement assessments.
rules.source_section: Section II.B — FDA's Benefit-Risk Framework
rules.verbatim_quote: CDER has integrated the Benefit-Risk Framework into its clinical review and decisional memo templates since 2015. In 2019, as part of the New Drugs Regulatory Program Modernization, CDER developed a new integrated review process and template for its marketing application (NDA and BLA) assessments. This template includes interdisciplinary, issue-based sections that highlight important issues and address their impact on benefit and risk. The template also presents the Benefit-Risk Framework as a component of section 1., Executive Summary.

rules.rule_text: FDA's benefit-risk assessment must address important considerations for each dimension of the Benefit-Risk Framework, including for the Benefit dimension: strengths/limitations of clinical trials; clinical relevance of study endpoints; nature of clinical benefits including effect size, time course, durability, and distribution of treatment effects; and generalizability to all populations likely to be prescribed the drug.
rules.source_section: Section III.A — Overview of Important Considerations (Table 1)
rules.verbatim_quote: Strengths/limitations of clinical trials, including design, and potential implications for assessing drug efficacy... Clinical relevance of the study endpoints: ability to measure or predict clinical outcomes of importance to patients... Description of the clinical benefits, including but not limited to: Nature of the effect (e.g., survival, slowing disease progression, reduction of serious outcomes, reduction of symptoms)... Effect size and associated uncertainty (e.g., a confidence interval), including an interpretation of clinical importance... Time course and durability of effect.

rules.rule_text: For the Risk and Risk Management dimension of the Benefit-Risk Framework, assessment must address: strengths/limitations of safety evidence; observed adverse events and safety signals including severity, likelihood, reversibility, and estimate of effect size; level of certainty for causal association; potential product quality issues; anticipated differences between clinical trial and postmarketing safety; potential for misuse or accidental exposure; and likely effectiveness of proposed risk management approaches.
rules.source_section: Section III.A — Overview of Important Considerations (Table 1)
rules.verbatim_quote: Strengths/limitations of the evidence regarding safety, and potential implications for assessing drug risks... Observed adverse events or safety signals and their clinical importance, including: Severity of the adverse event, the likelihood of its occurrence, reversibility, and the estimate of the effect size and its uncertainty... Ability to predict, monitor for, and/or prevent the adverse event... Potential for misuse or accidental exposure, and associated adverse consequences... Likely effectiveness of proposed approaches to managing risks.

rules.rule_text: For the Conclusions Regarding Benefit-Risk dimension, FDA considers: overall quality and strength of evidence; how therapeutic context affects assessment; relative importance of benefits and risks in the indicated population (including patient perspectives); time course over which benefits and risks occur; ability of patients and providers to assess the drug's benefit or risk; whether benefits and risks can be adequately communicated in product labeling; and whether postmarketing studies are necessary.
rules.source_section: Section III.A — Overview of Important Considerations (Table 1)
rules.verbatim_quote: Overall conclusions about the quality and strength of evidence and the remaining uncertainties regarding benefits and risks... Relative importance of the benefits and risks in the overall indicated population, which also considers patient perspectives... The time course over which the benefits and risks occur (e.g., considering adverse events that may occur shortly after initiation for benefits that may take years to accrue)... Whether the benefits and risks can be adequately communicated in product labeling to support informed individual benefit-risk assessments by patients and providers.

rules.rule_text: Uncertainties that can affect benefit-risk assessments include but are not limited to: limits on scientific understanding of the patient population and natural history; aspects of study design such as subgroup representation, choice of controls and endpoints; reliability of benefit/risk estimates due to statistical uncertainty or missing data; limited understanding of drug combination effects; untested risk management strategies; and limited patient input on disease burden and risk tradeoffs.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — The Impact of Uncertainty on Benefit-Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: Uncertainties that can affect benefit-risk assessments may include, but are not limited to: Limits on scientific understanding of the patient population and natural history of the condition... Aspects of the program or study design, such as the subgroup representation and diversity of the trial population, choice of controls, endpoints, and data sources... Reliability of the estimates of benefit or risk based upon variability in estimated effects due to sampling (i.e., statistical uncertainty), issues with trial conduct (e.g., missing data, poor study protocol compliance).

rules.rule_text: For drugs intended to treat serious diseases with unmet needs, FDA may accept greater uncertainties about benefit or risk at the time of approval, for example through the accelerated approval pathway. For drugs intended to treat non-serious diseases for which other therapeutic options exist, FDA would not be likely to accept as much uncertainty regarding either benefit or risk.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — The Impact of Uncertainty on Benefit-Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: For a drug intended to treat a serious disease with unmet needs, FDA may accept greater uncertainties about benefit or risk at the time of approval, for example through the accelerated approval pathway. In other situations, such as in the case of a drug that is intended to treat a non-serious disease and for which other therapeutic options exist, FDA would not be likely to accept as much uncertainty regarding either benefit or risk.
rules.exceptions: Accelerated approval pathway allows greater uncertainty for serious diseases with unmet needs
cross_references: FDA Guidance: Expedited Programs for Serious Conditions—Drugs and Biologics (May 2014)

rules.rule_text: For serious rare diseases, FDA may exercise regulatory flexibility by accepting clinical trials with smaller sample sizes. Accepting smaller sample sizes places even greater importance on maximizing the trial's potential to provide interpretable scientific evidence about the drug's benefits and risks by minimizing bias and maximizing precision with trial design features such as randomization, blinding, enrichment procedures, and adequate trial duration.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — The Impact of Uncertainty on Benefit-Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: In the case of serious rare diseases, FDA may thus exercise regulatory flexibility by accepting clinical trials that have smaller sample sizes. Accepting smaller sample sizes for serious rare diseases places even greater importance on maximizing the trial's potential to provide interpretable scientific evidence about the drug's benefits and risks in order to be respectful of patients' willingness to participate in clinical trials. Patient contribution is optimized in clinical trials (and particularly in small sample size studies) by minimizing bias and maximizing precision with trial design features such as randomization, blinding, enrichment procedures, and adequate trial duration.
rules.exceptions: Applies specifically to serious rare diseases where standard sample sizes cannot be achieved
cross_references: FDA Guidance: Rare Diseases: Common Issues in Drug Development (January 2019)

rules.rule_text: Patient experience data can inform nearly every aspect of FDA's benefit-risk assessment, including therapeutic context, potential benefits, acceptability of benefit-risk tradeoffs, value and burden of risk mitigation efforts, and information needs. If a methodologically sound and fit-for-purpose data collection tool is used, the collected data can provide direct evidence regarding the benefits and risks of the drug and their importance to patients.
rules.source_section: Section III.C — The Role of Patient Experience Data in FDA's Benefit-Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recognizes the importance of enabling meaningful patient input to inform drug development and regulatory decision-making, including in the context of FDA's benefit-risk assessment. Patients are experts in the experience of their disease or condition, and they are the ultimate stakeholders in the outcomes of medical treatment... If a methodologically sound and fit-for-purpose data collection tool(s) is used to collect patient experience data in a drug development program, the collected data can provide direct evidence regarding the benefits and risks of the drug and their importance to patients.
rules.exceptions: Patient experience data cannot overcome lack of substantial evidence of effectiveness

rules.rule_text: During premarket review, FDA indicates in review documentation whether relevant patient experience data are submitted as part of the application, and whether relevant information was not submitted in the application but has informed FDA review nonetheless.
rules.source_section: Section III.C — The Role of Patient Experience Data
rules.verbatim_quote: During premarket review, FDA indicates in review documentation whether relevant patient experience data are submitted as part of the application, and whether relevant information was not submitted in the application but has informed FDA review nonetheless.

rules.rule_text: Structured benefit-risk planning is a purposeful activity carried out by the sponsor to incorporate consideration of the product's benefit-risk assessment throughout the drug development lifecycle. Benefit-risk planning is most valuable in cases where a challenging benefit-risk assessment can be reasonably anticipated, either because the extent of benefit is expected to be modest or highly uncertain, or when serious adverse events can be anticipated.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — Structured Benefit-Risk Planning During Drug Development
rules.verbatim_quote: For the purposes of this guidance, structured benefit-risk planning is defined as a purposeful activity carried out by the sponsor to incorporate consideration of the product's benefit-risk assessment throughout the drug development lifecycle. Benefit-risk planning is most valuable in cases where a challenging benefit-risk assessment can be reasonably anticipated, either because the extent of benefit is expected to be modest or is highly uncertain, or when serious adverse events of the drug can be anticipated.

rules.rule_text: Benefit-risk planning activities should include: identifying patients more likely to experience greater benefit or less likely to experience serious adverse events; collecting sufficient dose-exposure-response data for both efficacy and safety/tolerability; selecting a primary efficacy endpoint that is a direct measure of how a patient feels, functions, or survives or a well-understood surrogate endpoint; and considering appropriate sample size for evaluating anticipated serious risks.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — Structured Benefit-Risk Planning During Drug Development
rules.verbatim_quote: Identification of patients (e.g., utilizing a predictive biomarker or other patient characteristics) who are more likely to experience greater expected benefit or less likely to experience serious adverse events... Collection of sufficient data throughout development to inform dose-exposure-response relationships for both efficacy and safety/tolerability... Selection of a primary efficacy endpoint that is a direct measure of how a patient feels, functions, or survives–or is a surrogate endpoint for which the relationship between an effect on the surrogate endpoint and the clinical outcome of interest is well understood.

rules.rule_text: Benefit-risk planning should include prospective collection of data to evaluate potential serious risks using targeted case report form prompts and/or independent adjudication, and implementation of appropriate risk mitigation measures into the clinical trial that can feasibly be implemented if the drug were approved, to provide sufficient evidence that risks can be adequately managed post-approval.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — Structured Benefit-Risk Planning During Drug Development
rules.verbatim_quote: Prospective collection of data to evaluate a potential serious risk, such as by actively ascertaining the occurrence and nature of the adverse event of interest using targeted case report form prompts and/or independent adjudication. Implementation of appropriate risk mitigation measures into the clinical trial with the ability to prevent or monitor for anticipated serious adverse events - in particular, measures that can feasibly be implemented if the drug were approved, to provide sufficient evidence that the risks can be adequately managed post-approval.

rules.rule_text: The End of Phase 2 (EOP2) meeting is typically a critical timepoint where discussions with FDA on benefit and risk considerations may be especially important and can influence the design of Phase 3 studies. Sponsors can add benefit-risk considerations as a proposed agenda item and provide specific questions and relevant supplementary information in the meeting package.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B — Appropriate Interactions Between a Sponsor and FDA
rules.verbatim_quote: The End of Phase 2 (EOP2) meeting is typically a critical timepoint where discussions with FDA on benefit and risk considerations may be especially important and can influence the design of phase 3 studies in ways that can enhance the characterization of the drug's benefits and risks, including decisions on study design, selection of appropriate patient populations, enrichment strategies, clinically meaningful endpoints, trial duration, dose-response assessment, and trial sizes. Sponsors can add 'benefit-risk considerations' as a proposed agenda item and provide specific questions and relevant supplementary information in the meeting package.
rules.exceptions: Earlier meetings may be valuable for rare/serious diseases or pediatric populations
cross_references: FDA Draft Guidance: Formal Meetings Between the FDA and Sponsors or Applicants of PDUFA Products (December 2017)

rules.rule_text: Patient preference information (PPI) collected for regulatory submission should be collected through a formal study with pre-specified protocols and analysis plans and should include a broad and representative sample of patients. PPI may be best suited to inform regulatory decision-making when: (1) significant risks of treatment or uncertainty about risks exist relative to expected benefits; (2) patients' views about most important benefits and risks vary considerably within a population; and/or (3) patients' views as to the most important benefits are expected to differ from those of healthcare professionals.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C — Collecting Patient Experience Data During Development
rules.verbatim_quote: When included in a regulatory submission, PPI should be collected through a formal study with pre-specified protocols and analysis plans and should include a broad and representative sample of patients... PPI may be best suited to inform regulatory decision-making when: (1) significant risks of treatment or uncertainty about risks exist relative to the expected benefits; (2) patients' views about the most important benefits and risks vary considerably within a population; and/or (3) when patients' views as to the most important benefits are expected to differ from those of healthcare professionals.
rules.exceptions: PPI would not overcome lack of substantial evidence of effectiveness
cross_references: FDA Patient-Focused Drug Development guidance series

rules.rule_text: When conducting additional benefit-risk analyses (e.g., for complex tradeoffs or significant uncertainties), sponsors should provide documentation including scientific justification for selection of methodology, plans for data collection, and analysis plans. When final analysis results are submitted, the documentation should include sufficient information allowing FDA to evaluate the strength of the methodology and the quality of the data, and to verify the analysis results.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D — Conducting Additional Analyses to Inform Benefit-Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: Generating rigorous evidence to inform FDA's benefit-risk assessment calls for careful planning and should involve prospective interaction with FDA early in the development of the analysis plan... sponsors are recommended to provide documentation including scientific justification for selection of methodology, plans for data collection, and analysis plans. When final analysis results are submitted, the documentation should include sufficient information allowing FDA to evaluate the strength of the methodology and the quality of the data, and to verify the analysis results.
rules.exceptions: Additional analyses are not required in all circumstances

rules.rule_text: As part of an NDA submission, the sponsor must provide an integrated summary of the benefits and risks of the drug, including a discussion of why the benefits exceed the risks under the conditions stated in labeling. This requirement is codified at 21 CFR 314.50(d)(5)(viii).
rules.source_section: Section IV.E — Presenting Benefit-Risk Considerations in the Marketing Application
rules.verbatim_quote: A critical source of benefit-risk information is the sponsor's NDA or BLA. As part of an NDA submission, the sponsor must provide '[a]n integrated summary of the benefits and risks of the drug, including a discussion of why the benefits exceed the risks under the conditions stated in labeling' (see 21 CFR 314.50(d)(5)(viii)).

rules.rule_text: The integrated summary of benefits and risks in the marketing application should include: a summary of important benefits and risks identified in the development program with discussion of their clinical importance; estimates of statistical uncertainty around benefit and risk magnitudes (e.g., confidence intervals); discussion of additional sources of uncertainty; potential differences between clinical trial and real-world use; and in some instances a graphical or tabular summary of benefits alongside risks.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E — Presenting Benefit-Risk Considerations in the Marketing Application
rules.verbatim_quote: Summary of the important benefits and risks identified in the development program and a description of the clinical importance of those benefits and risks... Estimates of the statistical uncertainty around the magnitudes of the most important benefits and risks (e.g., with confidence intervals)... Discussion of additional sources of uncertainty about benefits and/or risks (e.g., untested risk management strategies)... Potential differences between aspects of the clinical trial and expected real-world use (e.g., population, adherence, safety monitoring).

rules.rule_text: Benefit-risk assessment does not end with FDA approval. FDA considers a drug's benefit-risk assessment over the drug's lifecycle, acknowledging that understanding of both benefits and risks often changes over time as new information becomes available. When FDA considers benefits and risks in the postmarket setting, it does so in light of new information that is available post-approval.
rules.source_section: Section V — Benefit-Risk Assessment in the Postmarket Setting
rules.verbatim_quote: Benefit-risk assessment does not end with FDA's approval of a drug. FDA considers a drug's benefit-risk assessment over the drug's lifecycle, acknowledging that understanding of both the drug's benefits and risks often changes over time as new information about the product's effectiveness or safety becomes available. When FDA considers a drug's benefits and risks and uncertainties in the postmarket setting, it does so in light of new information that is available post-approval.

rules.rule_text: FDA may conduct a structured benefit-risk assessment in the postmarket setting when new information warrants reexamination of the marketed drug's benefit-risk. This can inform regulatory decisions including addition, modification, or release of a REMS; initiation or release of postmarketing study requirements; requesting or requiring labeling changes (e.g., addition, revision, or removal of a boxed warning); and, rarely, marketing withdrawal.
rules.source_section: Section V — Benefit-Risk Assessment in the Postmarket Setting
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA may conduct a structured benefit-risk assessment, guided by the Benefit-Risk Framework (see section II.B), when new information emerges that warrants a reexamination of the benefit-risk assessment of the marketed drug under the current requirements for approval. Examples of regulatory decisions that may be informed by such assessments include addition, modification, or release of a REMS, initiation or release of postmarketing study requirements, requesting or requiring labeling changes (e.g., addition, revision, or removal of a boxed warning), and, rarely, marketing withdrawal.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors should not wait for a periodic safety update to report a potentially serious safety concern. New information about a potential serious safety concern that could have an impact on a drug's benefit-risk profile should be communicated promptly to FDA.
rules.source_section: Section V — Benefit-Risk Assessment in the Postmarket Setting
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors, however, should not wait for a periodic safety update to report a potentially serious safety concern. New information about a potential serious safety concern that could have an impact on a drug's benefit-risk profile should be communicated promptly to FDA.

rules.rule_text: For drugs with serious risks that are identified or expected, circumstances that may support a favorable benefit-risk determination include: demonstrating direct and meaningful benefit on important clinical outcomes for a serious or life-threatening disease; representing a specific important advantage over available therapies; demonstrating that adequate risk mitigation measures can be implemented postmarket; identifying a subpopulation for whom benefits outweigh risks; or determining that a lower dose maintains meaningful benefit with a more acceptable risk profile.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — Regulatory Background
rules.verbatim_quote: Demonstrating direct and meaningful benefit of the drug on the most important clinical outcomes for a serious or life-threatening disease or condition. Determining that the drug represents a specific important advantage over currently available therapies. Demonstrating that adequate measures can be implemented to mitigate risks in the postmarket setting. Identifying a subpopulation (e.g., characterized by age, disease severity, genetic, pathophysiologic, or medical history) for whom the benefits outweigh the risks even if they do not do so in a broader population. Determining that a lower dose of the drug, compared to a higher dose, maintains meaningful benefit to patients, with a more acceptable risk profile.

rules.rule_text: For controlled substances, FDA's benefit-risk assessment incorporates broader public health considerations including risks related to misuse or accidental exposure in the intended population and in other populations who may have access to the drug.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — Regulatory Background
rules.verbatim_quote: For drugs identified as controlled substances, FDA's benefit-risk assessment incorporates considerations such as risks related to misuse or accidental exposure in the intended population and in other populations who may have access to the drug.

rules.rule_text: When the product labeling would need to adequately describe benefits and risks, including differences in response across approved subpopulations, to facilitate individual benefit-risk treatment decision-making by healthcare providers and patients, the labeling requirements must capture those distinctions to support a favorable benefit-risk assessment.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — Regulatory Background
rules.verbatim_quote: In such a situation, the product labeling would need to adequately describe the benefits and risks, including the differences in response across the approved subpopulations, as appropriate, thereby facilitating individual benefit-risk treatment decision-making by healthcare providers and patients.

rules.rule_text: Periodic reporting is an important mechanism for sponsors to communicate information to FDA that can inform benefit-risk assessment over the drug's lifecycle. If sponsors wish to submit a Periodic Benefit-Risk Evaluation Report (PBRER), FDA recommends following the format described in the most current version of the ICH E2C guidance for industry.
rules.source_section: Section V — Benefit-Risk Assessment in the Postmarket Setting
rules.verbatim_quote: Periodic reporting is an important mechanism for sponsors to communicate information to FDA that can inform benefit-risk assessment over the drug's lifecycle... If sponsors wish to submit a PBRER, FDA recommends that sponsors follow the format described in the most current version of the ICH E2C guidance for industry.
rules.exceptions: PBRER submission requires waiver under 21 CFR 314.90(a) or 600.90(a)
cross_references: FDA Guidance: Providing Postmarketing Periodic Safety Reports in the ICH E2C(R2) Format (November 2016)
